ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mee goreng; closest parent 'noodle dish' (FOODON_00002700) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP for stir-fried noodles used as closest parent; no specific mee goreng code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for mee goreng or stir-fried noodle dishes at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of mee goreng seafood or chicken as typically served at a Singapore hawker centre or restaurant, approximately 400 g.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Goreng (Fried Noodles) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists mee goreng (fried noodles) with seafood and chicken variants. Values are adapted from the HPB Foodo mee goreng entry (approximately 150 kcal/100 g for the standard hawker version). Where HPB Foodo does not report a micronutrient, the value is set to -1. The restaurant version (Riverfront Seafood, Boat Quay) may differ slightly due to premium ingredients; values represent a best-estimate composite for the dish category.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried noodles / egg noodles
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mee goreng seafood/chicken specifically. GI of 49 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for stir-fried egg/wheat noodles and published values for fried noodle dishes (GI range 40–55 for yellow wheat noodles cooked by stir-frying). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (49 × 74.0) / 100 ≈ 36.3, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 18.5 × 4 = 74.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg/100 g; ~1920 mg per 400 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 400 g serving of mee goreng provides approximately 1920 mg sodium, representing about 83% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and avoid adding extra condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~36 per serving) from refined yellow wheat noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: The moderate glycemic load of approximately 36 per standard serving means mee goreng can cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion (200–250 g), pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and choose the chicken variant to reduce overall energy density.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from stir-frying oil and animal protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (~1920 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat (~5.6 g per serving) in mee goreng poses a moderate cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request less oil during preparation, and favour the seafood variant for its omega-3 content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mee Goreng Seafood or Chicken is a popular Singaporean stir-fried noodle dish made with yellow wheat noodles (also called Hokkien noodles or egg noodles) cooked in a hot wok with either seafood (typically prawns and squid) or chicken, egg, bean sprouts, tomato, chilli, garlic, onion, and a savoury sauce based on soy sauce and sometimes tomato ketchup or chilli paste. The dish is a staple of Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops (kopitiams), and is also served in casual restaurants. The noodles are cooked at high heat to achieve a characteristic smoky wok flavour (wok hei). The texture is soft and slightly chewy from the noodles, with crisp bean sprouts and tender protein. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly spicy, and slightly sweet from the sauce. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g serving provides approximately 608 kcal, 28.8 g protein, 23.2 g fat, and 74 g carbohydrate, with a high sodium content of approximately 1920 mg. The dish has a low-to-moderate glycemic index (~49) due to the dense wheat noodle matrix, but the glycemic load per serving is moderate-to-high (~36) due to the large carbohydrate portion. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease, while the moderate glycemic load warrants portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The seafood variant provides additional omega-3 fatty acids from prawns and squid, which may offer cardiovascular benefit when consumed in moderation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mee goreng?
Is mee goreng suitable for diabetics?

Can I eat mee goreng if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo mee goreng entries, which represent the standard hawker-centre version of the dish. The restaurant version (Riverfront Seafood, Boat Quay) at SGD 17.60 may use premium seafood ingredients that alter the exact macronutrient profile. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for stir-fried wheat noodle dishes, as no direct GI measurement for mee goreng seafood/chicken exists in the approved GI database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Micronutrients not reported by HPB Foodo for this dish category are set to -1: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all -1 as they are not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish. The food_group in the input JSON was listed as 'Meat and alternatives'; this has been corrected to 'Grains and staples' as the primary energy source is yellow wheat noodles. The cuisine tag in the input was 'Chinese'; this has been updated to 'Singaporean' as mee goreng is a Singaporean hawker dish with Malay and Indian-Muslim culinary influences. The dish is generally considered halal when prepared at halal-certified hawker stalls; the is_halal flag is set to true as a general indicator but may vary by vendor. The restaurant source (Riverfront Seafood, Boat Quay) is a seafood restaurant; the dish may use premium seafood ingredients not reflected in the HPB Foodo standard values.
